name: OpenText Fortify SAST API
what_it_does: Enterprise SAST platform with REST API (formerly Micro Focus Fortify, now OpenText Fortify) — enabling AppSec teams to scan source code for security vulnerabilities across 27+ languages, manage security findings via Fortify Software Security Center (SSC), integrate with CI/CD pipelines via ScanCentral, and enforce security policy gates in enterprise development workflows.
use_cases: Scanning enterprise codebases for security vulnerabilities via Fortify SCA in CI/CD pipelines
Managing security findings via Fortify SSC REST API for enterprise AppSec programs
Integrating Fortify scans into Jenkins/Azure DevOps via ScanCentral for distributed scanning
Auditing and triaging security vulnerabilities via Fortify SSC API for security team workflows
Enforcing security policy compliance via Fortify SSC for release gating decisions
not_for: SCA/open-source dependency scanning (use Black Duck or Snyk for SCA — different product)
Small teams or startups without enterprise AppSec budgets (very expensive)
Teams preferring open-source SAST (use Semgrep or CodeQL for open-source alternatives)
best_when: A large enterprise needs comprehensive SAST across 27+ languages with enterprise policy management, audit trails, and integration into regulated software development processes.
avoid_when: You need affordable SAST, SCA, or prefer open-source tools — Fortify is enterprise-only with opaque pricing.

auth.notes: CIToken (Continuous Integration Token) generated from SSC user account. Authorization: FortifyToken {base64token} header. Also supports HTTP Basic auth. Token generated via /api/v1/tokens endpoint (POST with credentials). Token-based for CI/CD; role-based access control in SSC. REST API at https://{ssc-host}:{port}/ssc/api/v1/. On-premise deployment — your company's SSC server URL. No OAuth2. No webhooks.

pricing.paid_tiers: Enterprise license: custom pricing (OpenText quote); typically $100K-$1M+/year
pricing.notes: HP Fortify Software → Micro Focus Fortify (2017) → OpenText Fortify (2023). Ownership: HP → Micro Focus → OpenText (via $6B Micro Focus acquisition). 27+ supported languages: Java, C/C++, C#, JavaScript, Python, PHP, Ruby, Go, Swift, Kotlin, COBOL, and more. Components: SCA (Static Code Analyzer, CLI), SSC (Software Security Center, web server), ScanCentral (cloud-scale scanning). Fortify on Demand (FoD): hosted/SaaS version. CWE/SANS Top 25 mapping. Issue suppression and audit workflow. Dataflow tracing for vulnerability path visualization. IDE plugins (Eclipse, IntelliJ, VS Code). SAST industry pioneer — 20+ years. PCI DSS, SOX compliance support.

performance.rate_limits: On-premise — no external rate limits; scan time scales with codebase

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: FortifyToken or HTTP Basic. On-premise SSC URL varies. RBAC. Enterprise pricing opaque. OpenText ownership (post-Micro Focus). No webhooks — polling. 27+ languages. No free tier. Multiple ownership changes.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Issue reads idempotent by issue instance ID. Project/version reads idempotent. Artifact (scan) reads idempotent by artifact ID. Triage operations idempotent by issue ID.
known_agent_gotchas: Self-hosted SSC: base URL is your company's server (e.g., https://fortify.company.com/ssc)
CIToken: generate via /api/v1/tokens (POST credentials) → base64-encode for FortifyToken header
Multiple components: SCA (scan CLI), SSC (web server/API), ScanCentral (distributed) — different URLs
Fortify on Demand (FoD) is the SaaS version — different API endpoints from on-premise SSC
Project → Application Version hierarchy: all findings attached to specific application versions
Rebranded: HP Fortify → Micro Focus → OpenText — check current vendor support channel

security_score_components.security_notes: TLS enforced (self-hosted customer responsibility). FortifyToken or HTTP Basic. RBAC for users. SOC2/ISO27001/PCI-DSS/SOX compliance support. Source code analyzed locally on-premise — sensitive IP stays in-house. On-premise deployment for air-gapped environments. Multiple ownership changes (HP → Micro Focus → OpenText) introduce vendor risk. 20+ year old product with legacy auth patterns.
